sections.heading: Preconfigured Security Features
sections.body: The following security features are part of the Atlas product:

sections.heading: Encryption in Transit
sections.body:
Atlas requires TLS (Transport Layer Security)/SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) to encrypt the connections to your
databases.

TLS (Transport Layer Security)/SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) certificates are:

- Valid for 90 days from the day Atlas issues the certificate.

- Rotated 42 days before the certificate's expiration date.

To learn more about TLS encryption, see the [Atlas Security White Paper](https://www.mongodb.com/resources/products/capabilities/mongodb-atlas-security?tck=docs).

To configure SSL or TLS OCSP certificate revocation checking, see
[OCSP Certificate Revocation Check](oscp-cert-check).

sections.heading: Virtual Private Cloud
sections.body:
All Atlas projects with one or more M10+ dedicated clusters
receive their own dedicated VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) (or VNet if you use Azure (Microsoft Azure)).
Atlas deploys all dedicated clusters inside this VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) or VNet.

sections.heading: Encryption at Rest
sections.body:
By default, Atlas encrypts all data stored on your Atlas clusters.
Atlas also supports [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption).

sections.heading: FIPS (Federal Information Processing Standard Publication) 140-2
sections.body:
Atlas disables FIPS (Federal Information Processing
Standard Publication) 140-2 Level 2 for all databases by default.
If your environment requires that you enable it in Atlas, use MongoDB Atlas for Government.

MongoDB Atlas for Government automatically enables FIPS (Federal Information
Processing Standard Publication) 140-2 Level 2 for all databases.

sections.heading: Required Security Features
sections.body: You *must* configure the following security features:

sections.heading: Network and Firewall Requirements
sections.body:
Make sure your application can reach your MongoDB Atlas
environment. To add the inbound network access from your application environment to Atlas, do one of the following:

- Add the public IP addresses to your IP access list

- Use [VPC/VNet peering](vpc-peering) to add private IP
  addresses.

- Add [private endpoint connections](private-endpoint).

> **See Also:** [IP Access List](security-ip-access-list)

If your firewall blocks outbound network connections, you must also
open outbound access from your application environment to Atlas.
You must configure your firewall to allow your applications to make
outbound connections to ports 27015 to 27017 to TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) traffic on
the hostnames or IP addresses of your cluster.
This grants your applications access to databases stored on Atlas.

To obtain the current list of cluster IP addresses, use the
[Return All IP Addresses for One Project endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupipaddresses)
of the Atlas Administration API.

```
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer <access-token>" \
     --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2025-03-12+json" \
     --request GET \
     "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{GROUP-ID}/ipAddresses"
```

> **See Also:** [create a new API Key](create-project-api-key)

> **Note:** By default, MongoDB Atlas clusters do not need to be able to
> initiate connections to your application environments. If you wish
> to enable Atlas clusters with
> [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization),
> you must allow network access from Atlas clusters directly to
> your secure LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol). You can allow access to your LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) by using
> public or private IPs as long as a public DNS (Domain Name System) hostname points to
> an IP that the Atlas clusters can access.

If you are not using [VPC/VNet peering](vpc-peering) and plan
to connect to Atlas using public IP addresses, see the following
pages for additional information:

- [Can I specify my own VPC for my MongoDB Atlas project?](faq-atlas-side-hostnames)

- [Do Atlas cluster's public IPs ever change?](faq-public-ip-changes)

sections.heading: IP Access List
sections.body:
Atlas only allows client connections to the cluster from entries in
the project's IP access list. To connect, you must add an entry to the
IP access list. To set up the IP access list for the project, see
Configure IP Access List Entries.

For Atlas clusters deployed on [Google Cloud Platform (GCP)](google-gcp) or
[Microsoft Azure](microsoft-azure), add the IP (Internet Protocol) addresses of your Google Cloud or
Azure (Microsoft Azure) services to Atlas project IP (Internet Protocol) access list to grant
those services access to the cluster.

sections.heading: User Authentication or Authorization
sections.body:
Atlas requires clients to authenticate to connect to the database.
You must create database users to access the database. To set up
database users to your clusters, see
Configure Database Users. Atlas offers many
security features for [cluster authentication and authorization](db-deployment-auth).

To access clusters in a project, users must belong to
that project. Users can belong to multiple projects.

> **See Also:** [Configure Access to the Atlas UI](atlas-access).

sections.heading: Optional Security Features
sections.body: You *may* configure the following security features:

sections.heading: Network Peering Connection
sections.body:
Atlas supports peering connections with other AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), or
Google Cloud network peering connections. To learn more, see
Set Up a Network Peering Connection.

> **Important:** If this is the first `M10+` dedicated paid cluster for the
> selected region or regions *and* you plan on creating one or more
> VPC peering connections, please review the documentation
> on VPC peering connections before continuing.

sections.heading: Private Endpoints
sections.body:
Atlas supports private endpoints on:

- AWS (Amazon Web Services) using the [AWS PrivateLink](https://aws.amazon.com/privatelink/)
  feature

- Azure (Microsoft Azure) using the [Azure Private Link](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/private-link/) feature

- Google Cloud using the [GCP Private Service Connect](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-service-connect)
  feature

To use private endpoints, see Learn About Private Endpoints in Atlas.

sections.heading: Unified AWS Access
sections.body:
Some Atlas features, including [federated database instances](atlas-data-federation) and Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management, use AWS (Amazon Web Services)
[IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use.html)
for authentication.

To set up an AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role for Atlas to use, see
[Set Up Unified AWS Access](set-up-unified-aws-access).

sections.heading: Cluster Authentication and Authorization
sections.body:
Atlas offers the following security features for
cluster authentication and authorization.

sections.heading: Database User Authentication or Authorization
sections.body:
Atlas requires clients to authenticate to access
clusters. You must create database users to access the
database. To set up database users for your clusters,
see [Configure Database Users](mongodb-users).

sections.heading: Custom Roles for Database Authorization
sections.body:
Atlas supports creating [custom roles](mongodb-roles)
for database authorization in cases where the built-in Atlas
[built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) don't grant your desired set of
privileges.

sections.heading: Authentication with AWS IAM
sections.body:
You can set up authentication and authorization for your AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management)
roles. To learn more, see [AWS IAM Authentication](set-up-pwdless-auth).

sections.heading: User Authentication or Authorization with LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
sections.body:
Atlas supports performing user authentication and authorization
with LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol). To use LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol), see
Set Up User Authentication and Authorization with LDAP.

sections.heading: User Authentication with X.509
sections.body:
X.509 client certificates provide database users access to the
clusters in your project. Options for X.509
authentication include Atlas-managed X.509 authentication and
self-managed X.509 authentication. To learn more
about self-managed X.509 authentication, see [self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).

sections.heading: Restrict MongoDB Support Access to Atlas Backend Infrastructure
sections.body:
Organization owners can restrict MongoDB Production Support Employees
from accessing Atlas backend infrastructure for any Atlas
cluster in their organization. Organization owners may
grant a 24 hour bypass to the access restriction at the Atlas
cluster level.

> **Important:** Blocking infrastructure access from MongoDB Support
> may increase support issue response and resolution
> time and negatively impact your cluster's availability.

To enable this option, see Configure MongoDB Support Access to Atlas Backend Infrastructure.

sections.heading: Encryption at Rest using your Key Management
sections.body:
Atlas supports using AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service), Azure Key Vault, and Google Cloud to
encrypt storage engines and cloud provider backups. To use encryption
at rest, see Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management.

sections.heading: Client-Side Field Level Encryption
sections.body:
Atlas supports [client-side field level encryption](manual/core/security-client-side-encryption), including
[automatic](manual/core/security-automatic-client-side-encryption)
encryption of fields. All Atlas users are entitled to use MongoDB's
automatic client-side field level encryption features.

To learn more, see
[Client-Side Field Level Encryption Requirements](security/client-side-field-level-encryption-guide).

> **Note:** [MongoDB Compass](compass), the [Atlas UI](atlas-ui), and the [MongoDB Shell](mongodb-shell)
> (`mongosh`) do not support decrypting client-side field
> level-encrypted fields.

> **See Also:** 
>
> - [Client-Side Field Level Encryption](manual/core/security-client-side-encryption)
>
> - [Use-Case Guide for Client-Side Field Level Encryption](security/client-side-field-level-encryption-guide)

sections.heading: Database Auditing
sections.body:
Atlas supports auditing all system event actions. To use database
auditing, see Set up Database Auditing.

sections.heading: Access Tracking
sections.body:
Atlas surfaces authentication logs directly in the Atlas UI so
that you can easily review successful and unsuccesful authentication
attempts made against your clusters. To view your
database access history, see View Database Access History.

sections.heading: Multi-Factor Authentication for Atlas UI Access
sections.body:
Atlas supports MFA (multi-factor authentication) to help you control access to your
Atlas accounts. To set up MFA (multi-factor authentication), see [enable multi-factor authentication](atlas-enable-mfa).

sections.heading: Allow Access to or from the Atlas Control Plane
sections.body:
If you use any of the following Atlas features, you might have to
add Atlas IP addresses to your network's IP access list:

- [Alert Webhooks](third-party-integrations)

- [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption)

  > **Note:** If you enable the [Encryption at Rest](scale-cluster-enable-encryption)
  > feature, you must allow access from public IPs for all your hosts
  > in your deployment, including [Replica Set Config Servers](manual/core/sharded-cluster-config-servers) if you are using [sharded cluster](manual/reference/glossary).

Send a GET request to the `controlPlaneIPAddresses` endpoint
to fetch the current Atlas control plane IP addresses. The
[API endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listcontrolplaneipaddresses)
returns a list of inbound and outbound Atlas control plane IP
addresses in CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation categorized by cloud provider and region,
similar to the following:

```json
{
  "inbound":{
    "aws":{
      "<region-name>":["<IP-address>", ...],
      ...
    },
    "azure":{
      "<region-name>":["<IP-address>", ...],
      ...
    },"gcp":{
      "<region-name>":["<IP-address>", ...]
      ...
    }
  },
  "outbound":{
    "aws":{
      "<region-name>":["<IP-address>", ...],
      ...
    },
    "azure":{
      "<region-name>":["<IP-address>", ...],
      ...
    },
    "gcp":{
      "<region-name>":["<IP-address>", ...],
      ...
    }
  }
}
```

> **Important:** The Atlas Administration API uses the terms `inbound` and `outbound`
> in relation to the control plane, not your network. As a result:
>
> - Your network's **inbound** rules must match the `outbound`
>   CIDRs listed in the Atlas Administration API.
>
> - Your network's **outbound** rules must match the `inbound`
>   CIDRs listed in the Atlas Administration API.

The following diagram shows the relationship between `inbound` and
`outbound` for the control plane and your network:

![A diagram showing that inbound traffic for the control plane
reflects outbound traffic from your network, and outbound traffic for the
control plane reflects inbound traffic to your network.](/_includes/images/ControlPlaneInboundVsOutbound.svg)

To add the returned IP addresses to your cloud provider's KMS IP access list,
see the prerequisites for managing customer keys with [AWS](aws-ksm-prereqs),
[Azure](azure-kms-prereqs), and [GCP](gcp-kms-prereqs).

`controlPlane.outbound` lists the IP addresses of traffic coming from the
control plane. Your network's **inbound** HTTP IP address list must allow
access from the IP addresses listed in `controlPlane.outbound`.

We recommend that you [use the Atlas Admin API](atlas-fetch-control-plane-ips) to fetch the current outbound Atlas
control plane IP addresses.

`controlPlane.inbound` lists the IP addresses traffic coming into
the control plane. If your network allows **outbound** HTTP requests only to
specific IP addresses, you must allow access to the IP addresses listed in
`controlPlane.inbound` so that Atlas can communicate with your webhooks
and KMS (Key Management Service).

We recommend that you [use the Atlas Admin API](atlas-fetch-control-plane-ips)
to fetch the current inbound Atlas control plane IP addresses.

sections.heading: Allow Access to Data Federation
sections.body:
If your network allows outbound HTTPS requests only to specific IP addresses,
you must allow access from the inbound IP addresses on TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) port 27017
so that your requests can reach the federated database instance. We recommend
that you [use the Atlas Admin API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listcontrolplaneipaddresses)
to fetch the current inbound Atlas control plane IP addresses.

sections.heading: OCSP Certificate Revocation Check
sections.body:
If your network allows outbound requests to specific IP addresses only,
to allow SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) or TLS (Transport Layer Security) OCSP (Online Certificate Status
Protocol) certificate revocation checking, you must allow access to
Atlas' CA (Certificate Authority) OCSP (Online Certificate
Status Protocol) Responder servers that can be found in the
OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) URL (Uniform Resource Locator) of the SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) or
TLS (Transport Layer Security) certificate.

To disable OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)
certificate revocation checking, refer to the documentation
for the [MongoDB driver](drivers) version that your application uses.
